en.extract:
The yellow-spotted honeyeater (Meliphaga notata) is a species of bird in the family Meliphagidae. It is also known as the lesser lewin. The bird is endemic to northern Queensland. The bird's common name refers to the yellow patch that members of the species have behind their eyes.
The yellow-spotted honeyeater is olive, brown, and gray in colour. The bird's weight ranges from around 23 to 30 grams, and the wingspan ranges from about 8 to 9 centimeters. The species contains two subspecies, which are known as Meliphaga notata notata and Meliphaga notata mixta. Yellow-spotted honeyeaters are aggressive and have a loud and metallic call.


Taxonomy
The yellow-spotted honeyeater belongs to the order Passeriformes and the family Meliphagidae. The species may consist of two subspecies: Meliphaga notata notata and Meliphaga notata mixta. The former was described by Gould in 1867 and the latter by Matthews in 1912. The specific epithet notata derives from the Latin notatus, meaning 'spotted' or 'marked'.
A bird similar to the yellow-spotted honeyeater was described in 1844.


Description
The yellow-spotted honeyeater is olive-brown on the top and olive-gray below. However, there are brighter yellow areas on the bird's head. The bird has brown legs, feet and eyes; the beak is also brown. It is 16 to 20 cm (6.3 to 7.9 in) in size.
The mass of male yellow-spotted honeyeaters ranges from 24 to 29.5 grams, and averages at 27 g. Females range from 23.5 to 30 g, and average at 25.9 g.
For the subspecies Meliphaga notata notata, the average wingspan is 8.6 to 9.1 cm (3.4 to 3.6 in) for males and  7.9 to 8.3 cm (3.1 to 3.3 in) for females. For the subspecies Meliphaga notata mixta, the average wingspan is 8.3 to 8.7 cm (3.3 to 3.4 in) for males and  7.7 to 8.1 cm (3.0 to 3.2 in) for females.


Habitat
The bird largely lives in the tropical rainforests of North Queensland in extreme northeastern Australia, between Mackay and northern Cape York Peninsula. In 1901, it was also said to inhabit New Guinea.
Yellow-spotted honeyeaters typically live at elevations of 200 metres (660 ft) or greater. They have been observed at elevations as high as 1,200 m (3,900 ft) above sea level, although they are not often observed above 500 m (1,600 ft). While the birds typically live in forests, they have been known to visit suburbs with sufficient tree cover. They have even been observed in some cities that use local plants for landscaping. The species lives in both open forests and forests with an understory of shrubs.
The yellow-spotted honeyeater co-exists with the graceful honeyeater.


Population status
The yellow-spotted honeyeater is in the least concern category of the IUCN Red List. The total population of the species is apparently stable. The size of its range is over 110,000 square kilometers.


Behaviour
The call of the yellow-spotted honeyeater has been described as a metallic, high-pitched and "rattling song consisting of four to five 'ee-yeu' repetitions". The bird's call has also been said to sound like a "machine gun rattle". It eats insects, nectar, and fruit. Its body is partially horizontal when perching.
The yellow-spotted honeyeater is loud and aggressive. It tends to be solitary, or live in groups of two or, at most, several birds. It does not migrate.


Breeding
The nest of the yellow-spotted honeyeater is cup-shaped, usually secured at three points, and made of interwoven bark and light plant material. It often nests in bushes. However, it also nests in trees. The nest is usually 4.4 cm (1.7 in) to 7.6 cm (3.0 in) deep and 8.9 cm (3.5 in) to 10.2 cm (4.0 in) across. The actual area of the nest where the eggs are kept ranges from 3.8 cm (1.5 in) to 5.1 cm (2.0 in) deep and 6.4 cm (2.5 in) to 7.6 cm (3.0 in) across.
The eggs of the yellow-spotted honeyeater range between inclined and elliptical and have a glossy surface. While most of the egg is typically white, there are some purple or brown patches at the top of it. The eggs usually are laid two at a time, but are sometimes laid three at a time.
The birds breed between August and January. Young birds hatch after about two weeks of incubation, and leave the nest after another two weeks or so.
One generation for the birds lasts about 5.5 years.


See also
Lewin's honeyeater


References


External links
List of other members of Meliphagidae Archived 3 December 2013 at the Wayback Machine

The '''yellow-spotted honeyeater''' ('''''Meliphaga notata''''') is a species of bird in the family [[Meliphagidae]]. It is also known as the '''lesser lewin'''.<ref name="climatewatch">{{Citation|url = http://www.climatewatch.org.au/species/birds/yellow-spotted-honeyeater|title = Yellow-spotted Honeyeater|access-date = November 30, 2013|archive-date = 3 March 2021|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20210303154357/https://www.climatewatch.org.au/species/birds/yellow-spotted-honeyeater}}</ref> The bird is [[Endemism|endemic]] to northern [[Queensland]]. The bird's common name refers to the yellow patch that members of the species have behind their eyes.<ref>{{Citation|author1=Jeannie Gray |author2=Ian Fraser |url = https://books.google.com/books?id=z6ia8rqTa7EC&q=%22yellow-spotted+honeyeater%22&pg=PA198|title = Australian Bird Names: A Complete Guide|year = 2013|isbn=978-0-643-10470-9 |access-date = November 30, 2013}}</ref>

The yellow-spotted honeyeater is olive, brown, and gray in colour. The bird's weight ranges from around 23 to 30 grams, and the wingspan ranges from about 8 to 9 centimeters. The species contains two subspecies, which are known as ''Meliphaga notata notata'' and ''Meliphaga notata mixta''. Yellow-spotted honeyeaters are [[aggression|aggressive]] and have a loud and metallic call.

==Taxonomy==
The yellow-spotted honeyeater belongs to the order [[Passeriformes]] and the family [[Meliphagidae]].<ref name = "lochman"/><ref name = "lynxeds"/> The species may consist of two [[subspecies]]: ''Meliphaga notata notata'' and ''Meliphaga notata mixta''. The former was described by Gould in 1867 and the latter by Matthews in 1912.<ref name = "lynxeds">{{Citation|url = http://ibc.lynxeds.com/species/yellow-spotted-honeyeater-meliphaga-notata|title = Yellow-spotted Honeyeater (Meliphaga notata)|access-date = November 30, 2013}}</ref> The specific epithet ''notata'' derives from the Latin ''notatus'', meaning 'spotted' or 'marked'.<ref>Jobling, James A. (2010). The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names. London: Christopher Helm. p. 38. {{ISBN|978-1-4081-2501-4}}.</ref>

A bird similar to the yellow-spotted honeyeater was described in 1844.<ref name = "nestsandeggs"/>

==Description==
The yellow-spotted honeyeater is [[olive (color)|olive]]-brown on the top and olive-gray below. However, there are brighter yellow areas on the bird's head. The bird has brown legs, feet and eyes; the beak is also brown. It is {{convert|16|to|20|cm|in|abbr=on}} in size.<ref name = "climatewatch"/>

The mass of male yellow-spotted honeyeaters ranges from 24 to 29.5 grams, and averages at 27 g. Females range from 23.5 to 30 g, and average at 25.9 g.<ref>{{Citation|author = John B. Dunning Jr.|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=TcnOTPILlcEC&q=%22yellow-spotted+honeyeater%22&pg=PA461|title = CRC Handbook of Avian Body Masses|edition = Second|date= December 5, 2007|isbn = 978-1-4200-6445-2|access-date = November 30, 2013}}</ref>

For the subspecies ''Meliphaga notata notata'', the average [[wingspan]] is {{convert|8.6|to|9.1|cm|in|abbr=on}} for males and  {{convert|7.9|to|8.3|cm|in|abbr=on}} for females. For the subspecies ''Meliphaga notata mixta'', the average wingspan is {{convert|8.3|to|8.7|cm|in|abbr=on}} for males and  {{convert|7.7|to|8.1|cm|in|abbr=on}} for females.<ref>{{Citation|author1=R Schodde |author2=IJ Mason |url = https://books.google.com/books?id=XXbJoaGF36YC&q=%22yellow-spotted+honeyeater%22&pg=PT1255|title= Directory of Australian Birds: Passerines: Passerines|date = October 1, 1999|isbn=978-0-643-10293-4 |access-date = November 30, 2013}}</ref>

==Habitat==
The bird largely lives in the [[tropical rainforest]]s of [[North Queensland]] in extreme northeastern Australia, between [[Mackay, Queensland|Mackay]] and northern [[Cape York Peninsula]].<ref name = "whenwhere"/><ref name = "lochman">{{Citation|url = http://www.lochmantransparencies.com/products/birds-passerines-2/yellow-spotted-honeyeater-meliphaga-notata-llg-748/|title = Australian Wildlife / Birds - Passerines 2 / Yellow-spotted Honeyeater|access-date = November 30, 2013|archive-date = 18 October 2017|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20171018110855/http://www.lochmantransparencies.com/products/birds-passerines-2/yellow-spotted-honeyeater-meliphaga-notata-llg-748/}}</ref> In 1901, it was also said to inhabit [[New Guinea]].<ref name = "nestsandeggs">{{Citation|author = Archibald James Campbell|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=--lPAAAAYAAJ&q=%22yellow-spotted+honeyeater%22&pg=PR25|title =Nests and eggs of Australian birds: including the geographical distribution of the species and popular observations thereon, Volumes 1-2|year = 1901|access-date = November 30, 2013}}</ref>

Yellow-spotted honeyeaters typically live at elevations of {{convert|200|m|ft}} or greater.<ref name = "whenwhere"/> They have been observed at elevations as high as {{convert|1200|m|ft|abbr=on}} above sea level, although they are not often observed above {{convert|500|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name = "nestsandeggs"/><ref name = "fieldguide"/> While the birds typically live in forests, they have been known to visit [[suburb]]s with sufficient tree cover.<ref>{{Citation|author = Michael McCoy|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=fOEBHPFHgFMC&q=%22yellow-spotted+honeyeater%22&pg=PA212|title = Reef and Rainforest|date = October 1, 2010|isbn = 978-0-643-10212-5|access-date = November 30, 2013}}</ref> They have even been observed in some cities that use local plants for landscaping. The species lives in both open forests and forests with an [[understory]] of [[shrub]]s.<ref>{{Citation|author = John Chambers|url = http://rainforest-australia.com/yellow_honeyeater.htm|title = YELLOW HONEYEATER|access-date = November 30, 2013|archive-date = 27 October 2016|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20161027023711/http://rainforest-australia.com/yellow_honeyeater.htm}}</ref>

The yellow-spotted honeyeater co-exists with the [[graceful honeyeater]].<ref>{{Citation|author1=Richard Thomas |author2=Sarah Thomas |author3=David Andrew |author4=Alan McBride |url = https://books.google.com/books?id=6SLS2bZdwO0C&q=%22yellow-spotted+honeyeater%22&pg=PA341|title = The Complete Guide to Finding the Birds of Australia|date = February 23, 2011|isbn=978-0-643-10226-2 |access-date = November 30, 2013}}</ref>

===Population status===
The yellow-spotted honeyeater is in the [[least concern]] category of the [[IUCN Red List]]. The total population of the species is apparently stable. The size of its range is over 110,000 square kilometers.<ref>{{Citation|url = http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/speciesfactsheet.php?id=5269|title = LC: Yellow-spotted Honeyeater Meliphaga notata|year = 2013|access-date = November 30, 2013}}</ref>

==Behaviour==
The [[bird call|call]] of the yellow-spotted honeyeater has been described as a metallic, high-pitched and "rattling song consisting of four to five 'ee-yeu' repetitions".<ref name = "climatewatch"/><ref name = "evolved">{{Citation|author = Kim Sterelny|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=VOSwqLNBmmUC&q=%22yellow-spotted+honeyeater%22&pg=PT229|title = The Evolved Apprentice: How Evolution Made Humans Unique|date = January 1, 2012|isbn = 978-0-262-30049-0|access-date = November 30, 2013}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.graemechapman.com.au/library/viewphotos.php?c=283|title=Yellow-spotted Honeyeater - Australian Birds - photographs by Graeme Chapman|access-date=31 March 2020|archive-date=8 April 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200408173325/https://www.graemechapman.com.au/library/viewphotos.php?c=283|url-status=dead}}</ref> The bird's call has also been said to sound like a "[[machine gun]] rattle".<ref name = "evolved"/><ref name = "fieldguide"/> It eats [[insect]]s, [[nectar]], and [[fruit]].<ref name = "climatewatch"/> Its body is partially horizontal when perching.<ref name = "fieldguide">{{citation|author= Michael K. Morcombe|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=5XNFAQAAIAAJ&q=%22yellow-spotted+honeyeater%22|title = Field guide to Australian birds|year = 2003|isbn = 978-1-74021-417-9|access-date = November 30, 2013}}</ref>

The yellow-spotted honeyeater is loud and aggressive. It tends to be solitary, or live in groups of two or, at most, several birds.<ref name = "fieldguide"/> It does not [[Bird migration|migrate]].<ref name = "factsheet">{{Citation|url = http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/speciesfactsheet.php?id=5269&m=1|title =LC: Yellow-spotted Honeyeater Meliphaga notata|year = 2013|access-date = November 30, 2013}}</ref>

==Breeding==
The nest of the yellow-spotted honeyeater is cup-shaped, usually secured at three points, and made of interwoven [[tree bark|bark]] and light plant material.<ref name = "climatewatch"/><ref name = "nestsandeggs"/> It often nests in bushes.<ref name = "nestsandeggs"/> However, it also nests in [[tree]]s.<ref name = "whenwhere"/><ref name = "nestsandeggs"/> The nest is usually {{convert|4.4|cm|in|abbr=on}} to {{convert|7.6|cm|in|abbr=on}} deep and {{convert|8.9|cm|in|abbr=on}} to {{convert|10.2|cm|in|abbr=on}} across. The actual area of the nest where the eggs are kept ranges from {{convert|3.8|cm|in|abbr=on}} to {{convert|5.1|cm|in|abbr=on}} deep and {{convert|6.4|cm|in|abbr=on}} to {{convert|7.6|cm|in|abbr=on}} across.<ref name = "nestsandeggs"/>

The [[bird egg|eggs]] of the yellow-spotted honeyeater range between inclined and elliptical and have a [[Gloss (optics)|glossy]] surface. While most of the egg is typically white, there are some purple or brown patches at the top of it. The eggs usually are laid two at a time, but are sometimes laid three at a time.<ref name = "nestsandeggs"/>

The birds breed between August and January.<ref name = "whenwhere">{{Citation|url = http://www.climatewatch.org.au/species/birds/yellow-spotted-honeyeater#when_where|title = Yellow-spotted Honeyeater|access-date = November 30, 2013|archive-date = 3 March 2021|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20210303154357/https://www.climatewatch.org.au/species/birds/yellow-spotted-honeyeater#when_where}}</ref> Young birds hatch after about two weeks of [[Egg incubation|incubation]], and leave the nest after another two weeks or so.<ref name = "climatewatch"/>

One [[generation]] for the birds lasts about 5.5 years.<ref name = "factsheet"/>

==See also==
* [[Lewin's honeyeater]]
